title: Distributed Rate Limiter
problemStatement:
Design a distributed rate limiter that can be used to throttle API requests.

**Functional Requirements:**
- Limit the number of requests a user/client can make in a given time window
- Support different rate limits for different API endpoints
- Return appropriate HTTP 429 responses when limit is exceeded
- Support both per-user and global rate limits

**Non-Functional Requirements:**
- Very low latency (should not add significant overhead to requests)
- Highly available — if the rate limiter goes down, allow requests through
- Accurate counting even in a distributed environment

**Scale:**
- 10M requests per second across all services
- Thousands of API servers behind the rate limiter

requiredComponents: Rate limiting algorithm (Token Bucket / Sliding Window)
Configuration service for rate limit rules
keyTradeoffs: Token Bucket vs Leaky Bucket vs Fixed Window vs Sliding Window Log vs Sliding Window Counter
Centralized (Redis) vs local rate limiting with periodic sync
Hard rate limiting vs soft (with some tolerance for distributed lag)
Fail-open vs fail-closed when Redis is unavailable
antiPatterns: Using a relational database for rate limit counters
Fixed window counters that allow burst at window boundaries
Not handling clock drift across distributed servers
Fail-closed design that blocks all traffic when rate limiter is down

followUpTriggers.question: What happens if a user sends all their requests at the boundary between two windows?
If you have 100 API servers each checking rates independently, how do you ensure accurate global counts?
What happens to your API traffic if Redis goes down?

rubricFull.sampleAnswer: Implement a sliding window rate limiter using Redis with sorted sets. Each request adds a timestamped entry, and we count entries within the window using ZRANGEBYSCORE. For performance, use a hybrid approach: local in-memory counters synced to Redis periodically. Place the rate limiter as middleware in the API gateway. Use a token bucket algorithm for smoothing. Fail-open if Redis is unavailable. Store rate limit rules in a config service that can be updated without redeployment.

scoringDimensions.name: Algorithm Choice
scoringDimensions.criteria: Understanding of different rate limiting algorithms and appropriate selection

scoringDimensions.name: Distributed Design
scoringDimensions.criteria: Handling distributed counting accurately with Redis or similar

scoringDimensions.name: Integration
scoringDimensions.criteria: Where the rate limiter sits in the architecture (gateway, middleware, sidecar)

hints: Consider the trade-offs between different algorithms: Token Bucket allows bursts, Sliding Window is more precise.
Redis has atomic operations like INCR and EXPIRE that are useful here — think about how to use them together.
What should happen at the boundary of time windows? That's where fixed window counters have a well-known problem.
followUpQuestions: How would you implement tiered rate limits (free vs premium users)?
How would you rate limit by IP vs by authenticated user vs globally?
How would you handle rate limiting in a multi-region setup?

nodes.label:
API Gateway
(rate limiter middleware
runs here before routing)

Local In-Memory Counter
(per API server)
synced every 100ms

Redis Cluster
(sliding window sorted sets)
ZADD / ZCOUNT atomic ops

edges.label:
check local approx count first
(fast path, avoids Redis round-trip)

ZADD + ZCOUNT for exact count
(slow path on local threshold breach)

notes: Sliding window with sorted set: ZADD ratelimit:{userId}:{endpoint} {now_ms} {request_uuid}; ZREMRANGEBYSCORE 0 {now_ms - windowMs}; count = ZCARD — all in a single Lua script for atomicity
Hybrid approach: local counter handles ~80% of traffic without Redis; only syncs to Redis when local count approaches the limit threshold
Fail-open policy: if Redis is unreachable, allow the request through and log — availability > strict accuracy during outage
Fixed window double-spend attack: user can make 2× the limit by sending all requests at the end of window N and the start of window N+1 — sliding window eliminates this
Response headers to always include: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset (epoch seconds of window reset)
